seoTitle: Google Docs Read Aloud: Get Text-to-Speech Working
metaDescription: Learn how to get Google Docs to read your documents aloud using built-in accessibility tools, browser read-aloud features, and mobile options.
intro: If you'd rather listen to a document than stare at a screen, Google Docs has several ways to read your text back to you. This guide walks through turning on screen reader support, using browser-based read-aloud tools, and adjusting playback so you can proofread or review reports hands-free.

steps.heading: Turn on accessibility screen reader support
steps.body: Open your document's accessibility settings to activate native screen reader compatibility, which lets Google Docs announce text as you navigate.

steps.heading: Use keyboard shortcuts to read selected text
steps.body: Once accessibility support is active, keyboard shortcuts let you trigger read-aloud tracking over a selected paragraph or an entire page.

steps.heading: Try browser-based read-aloud tools
steps.body: Desktop browsers like Microsoft Edge (Read Aloud) and Google Chrome (Reading Mode) can read your document text fluidly without extra setup inside Docs itself.

steps.heading: Adjust playback speed and voice
steps.body: Use the text-to-speech customization controls to change reading speed, pause and resume, and switch between different voice profiles.

steps.heading: Listen on mobile
steps.body: On Android and iOS, mobile accessibility features like Select to Speak or Spoken Content let you listen to your Google Docs files directly in the app.

faq.question: Can Google Docs read my document out loud without extensions?
faq.answer: Yes, by enabling the built-in accessibility screen reader support or using a browser's native read-aloud feature, you don't need to install anything extra.

faq.question: Can I change the reading voice or speed?
faq.answer: Yes, the text-to-speech controls let you adjust playback speed and switch between available voice profiles.

faq.question: Does this work on my phone?
faq.answer: Yes, mobile accessibility tools such as Select to Speak on Android or Spoken Content on iOS can read your Google Docs files aloud in the app.

faq.question: Will it read tables and complex layouts correctly?
faq.answer: Complex layouts and tables can sometimes cause reading order issues, so it helps to check pronunciation and skip problematic sections manually if needed.
